Title: Anwar: RTD's digital shift not just an upgrade, but a 'necessity' for integrity
Article:
BATU KAWAN: The Road Transport Department (RTD) must spearhead digital reform as part of broader efforts to strengthen integrity, reduce public friction and meet rising expectations for public service delivery, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said.
Anwar said the agency's frontline enforcement role made it one of the most exposed government bodies, where every decision is scrutinised by the public — particularly in the era of social media.
"The RTD is a department that deals directly with the people. When enforcement is strict, it is unpopular. When it is lenient, there is criticism from other quarters. You are constantly under pressure from all sides," he said in his speech at the RTD's 80th anniversary celebration here today.
Anwar said this environment made digital transformation not just an administrative upgrade, but a necessity to safeguard transparency and rebuild trust.
"The step towards digitalisation is very appropriate because it reduces opportunities for allegations, abuse and manipulation.
"It strengthens transparency and protects officers who are carrying out their duties," he added.
He highlighted the department's 80-year history since its establishment in 1946, saying it had evolved into a stronger and more respected enforcement agency.
"We are grateful that RTD today stands as a firm and recognised institution in the country," he said.
The prime minister said Malaysia's development agenda under the Malaysia Madani framework required all public institutions to move away from outdated systems and adopt a culture of continuous improvement.
He said progressive nations do not remain with old methods.
"We must strive for improvement and excellence in everything we do," he said.
Anwar said rising public expectations, amplified by digital connectivity, meant government agencies must adapt quickly and responsibly.
"There is what we call a rising expectation among the people. The challenge is to respond with reform, discipline and better systems," he said.
He also praised ongoing reforms within the RTD, including improvements in governance and transparency, and commended the Transport Minister Anthony Loke Siew Fook for his commitment to defending the agency's integrity amid public scrutiny.
Touching on reforms such as the allocation of vehicle registration numbers, Anwar said more transparent mechanisms, including open tenders, should be embraced to ensure fairness and generate revenue for public benefit.
"If we improve transparency, the revenue can be strengthened and returned to the people or reinvested to improve the RTD's capacity," he said.
Anwar said the RTD's enforcement role covered all road users without exception, making consistency and professionalism essential in its operations.
"Everyone uses the roads. Many break the rules, and sometimes it leads to serious consequences. The RTD must enforce the law firmly but fairly," he added.
He said that the department's reputation had improved significantly over the years, reflecting sustained efforts to professionalise its operations.
"From my experience over the decades, I must acknowledge that the RTD's image today is much better than before. I congratulate all of you for that progress," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pas hopes new MACC chief's appointment will lead to significant reforms
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Pas has welcomed the appointment of the new Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner, saying it hopes it will lead to significant reforms.
The party's information chief, Ahmad Fadhli Shaari, said this was important in ensuring the agency remains respected and independent.
The Pasir Mas member of parliament also listed several cases for the next MACC chief, Datuk Seri Abdul Halim Aman, to tackle, including allegations concerning the existence of a "corporate mafia".
Other calls included an investigation into the share ownership of current chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki.
Ahmad Fadhli also proposed that cases involving allegations by Albert Tei, as revealed in the businessman's videos, be prioritised.
"All these issues should be investigated fairly and comprehensively, and the findings must be disclosed to the people," he said.
Earlier today, Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar announced Halim's appointment.
The former High Court judge's tenure is effective May 13. He replaces Azam, who has led the agency since 2020.
Azam has faced numerous calls to step down over share ownership and allegations of links to a purported "corporate mafia". He has denied any wrongdoing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: GOF detain fisherman for alleged diesel subsidy misuse
Article:
YAN: A man was detained by the General Operations Force (GOF) while filling drums with fuel at a petrol station here yesterday.
He is believed to be a part-time fisherman and is alleged to have used a subsidised diesel fleet card belonging to a family member to purchase diesel at the station.
The Kedah Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry (KPDN) said in a statement that enforcement officers from KPDN Alor Setar took custody of the suspect after his arrest by the GOF at the location.
"At the time of the arrest, the 29-year-old local man was filling diesel into blue drums placed at the rear of a lorry covered with canvas.
"Checks found that he had filled 150 litres of diesel into the drums without a special permit and had used a fleet card believed to belong to a family member.
"The case was handed over to KPDN Alor Setar for further investigation, and the total value of the seizure is RM50,880," the statement said.
KPDN Alor Setar has opened an investigation paper into the case, which is being probed under Section 21 of the Control of Supplies Act.
Those convicted face a fine of up to RM1 million, imprisonment of up to three years, or both, for a first offence.
Any complaints or information may be channelled via WhatsApp at 019-848 8000, the EZ ADU KPDN application, the KPDN hotline at 1-800-886-800, or the e-complaints portal at eaduan.kpdn.gov.my
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RON95 sales at border stations drop 20pct following police, GOF deployment
Article:
LANGKAWI: Sales of RON95 petrol have dropped by 20 per cent following the deployment of police and General Operations Force (GOF) personnel at 170 high-risk stations near the border.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail said that based on records from April 13 to 21, RON95 sales showed a significant decline, while RON97 sales recorded an increase.
"This development proves that the strategy implemented by the government is effective in curbing fuel leakages previously suspected of being misappropriated by irresponsible parties.
"This is among the successes of Op Tiris, where the police assisted enforcement officers from the Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry in monitoring petrol stations near the border," he said at a press conference following a working visit to the PGA Control Post at Teluk Ewa Jetty today.
He added that Op Tiris in Kedah recorded 58 seizures of various controlled goods during the first four months of this year.
"From January until last Wednesday, 58 seizures were made involving 33,586 units of goods with a total value of RM121,945.45.
"Among the items seized were diesel, petrol, liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), cooking oil, coarse sugar, and wheat flour, reflecting the variety of commodities targeted by smuggling activities," he said.
He noted that of that total, diesel seizures were the highest at RM101,676.20, followed by petrol (RM10,561.54), cooking oil (RM4,839.60), LPG (RM3,873.20), coarse sugar (RM668.07), and wheat flour (RM326.84).
"These statistics show that fuel remains the primary target for syndicates, especially in high-risk areas such as land and sea border routes in Kedah," he said.
During the same visit, he also inspected operations at the Telaga Harbour Immigration Office in Pantai Kok to ensure smooth service, besides identifying current needs and challenges faced by personnel.
The immigration post serves as an international entry and exit point via sea routes connecting Langkawi with regional destinations, specifically Koh Lipe, Thailand.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Heed PM's call, allow WFH, Ramanan tells bosses
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Private sector employers have been urged to heed Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's call to adopt flexible work arrangements, including work-from-home (WFH).
Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri R. Ramanan said many large corporations, multinationals, and banks have already adopted WFH practices.
"The call to consider flexible work arrangements and WFH practices should be implemented by private companies where possible.
"Amid economic uncertainty, the government is also striving to achieve the best possible savings," he told reporters after launching Vetri Madani in Brickfields.
Commenting on government plans to limit the movement of cabinet ministers and ministries to reduce costs, Ramanan said the matter was discussed about a month ago.
He said that he agreed with the move, adding that it was not a new development.
"The prime minister informed us of this during cabinet meetings. It applies not only to members of the administration but also to heads of agencies and departments," he said.
Some 200,000 civil servants are involved in the government's WFH arrangement, which began on April 15.
Earlier, Ramanan launched Vetri Madani, a training programme specifically for the Indian community. He said RM10 million has been allocated to train 2,000 participants in fields such as artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning, upskilling, and reskilling.
"The training will be conducted physically and through hybrid methods, ensuring it is accessible to those living further away. As places are limited, those interested should apply through the HRD Corp website at www.hrdcorp.gov.my," he said.
Ramanan encouraged the Indian community to seize the opportunity to improve their standard of living through the programme.
"Upon completion, we will integrate participants with MyFutureJobs. This is the first sub-immersive platform in Malaysia using AI for job matching. They will be provided with employment opportunities that best suit them," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Family hopes for miracle after Malaysian jailed in Japan
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Muhammad Faiq Zafran Mohd Jailani, 24, who is currently detained in Toyamanishi, Japan, was sentenced to four years' imprisonment by the Toyama District Court yesterday.
The lawyer appointed by his family, Datuk Noor Svetlana Mohd Noor Nordin, said the sentence was imposed for three charges and will run concurrently from the date of his arrest on November 18, 2025.
She said this means Faiq's sentence has been reduced from four years to three years and nine months.
"However, we will file a notice of appeal to the High Court for the next process. We also hope for a miracle for him, as there is a provision for suspended sentences under Article 25 of the Japanese Penal Code, under which he may be allowed to return to Malaysia on a conditional basis," she said during a live broadcast on TikTok after the trial.
Yesterday, Noor Svetlana and Faiq's father, Mohd Jailani, travelled to Japan for the second time to attend the hearing and sentencing.
Previously, media reported that Faiq was believed to have fallen victim to an online fraud syndicate operating in the country, which later led to his arrest in Toyama.
There was also misinformation surrounding his arrest, including initial reports that he was found with cash and gold worth about RM500,000.
Faiq was investigated last November under Article 246(1) of the Japanese Penal Code for fraud and Article 60 for offences committed jointly, which is comparable to Sections 420 and 34 of the Malaysian Penal Code.
Noor Svetlana said yesterday was the first time the father was able to speak to Faiq.
"So, whatever happens, we hope for a miracle and that he will be given a good behaviour bond, with his release made conditional.
"This is because he did not arrange everything; he was deceived and trusted his friend.
"Article 25 of the Japanese Penal Code is similar to the provision in Malaysia, but it would allow him to return to his country of origin," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US envoys to head to Pakistan Saturday for Iran talks
Article:
WASHINGTON: US envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner will head on Saturday to Pakistan for a new round of talks with Iran on ending the war, the White House said.
"I can confirm Special Envoy Witkoff and Jared Kushner will be off to Pakistan again tomorrow morning to engage in talks," White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt told Fox News on Friday.
"The Iranians reached out, as the president called on them to do, and asked for this in-person conversation," she said.
"We're hopeful that it will be a productive conversation and hopefully move the ball forward towards a deal."
The talks would come more than two weeks after Vice President JD Vance held a first round of talks with Iranian representatives in Pakistan.
Vance was ready earlier this week to fly back out but put his trip on hold after Iran did not confirm participation.
Leavitt said that Vance remained ready to fly to Pakistan but will not for now.
"Everyone will be on standby to fly to Pakistan if necessary," she said.
Witkoff, a Trump business associate turned globe-trotting negotiator, and Kushner, Trump's son-in-law, had been negotiating with Iran just before Israel and the United States launched attacks on February 28, leading Teheran to accuse them of deceit.
During the first round of talks in Pakistan, Vance said that Iran refused US demands on constraints on its contested nuclear programme.
Trump has indefinitely extended a ceasefire, after earlier issuing genocidal threats to destroy all Iranian civilisation.
"The president always wants to give diplomacy a chance. It's always his first option, and he's willing to do that here again," Leavitt said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Anwar: Crucial to maintain good ties with major powers amid global energy crisis
Article:
BATU KAWAN: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said it makes "a lot of sense" for Malaysia to continue establishing and maintaining good relations with other countries as global tensions threaten to disrupt economic stability and energy supplies.
Anwar said Malaysia's ability to maintain ties with major powers, Gulf states and Iran is critical as the conflict involving Israel, the United States and Iran intensifies, with spillover effects already impacting the wider Middle East and global markets.
He said the government remains committed to a non-aligned, independent approach — engaging all sides while upholding clear positions against violence and humanitarian violations.
"Malaysia is a free and sovereign nation. We do not interfere militarily, nor do we jeopardise our diplomatic ties, but we must speak out when there are grave injustices, including attacks on civilians," he said, adding that the cabinet hada taken a united stance in condemning violence in Iran, Gaza and Lebanon.
He said this in his speech at the Road Transport Department's 80th anniversary celebration here today.
The external turmoil comes despite Malaysia recording stronger-than-expected economic performance, including higher growth, record investments, improved ringgit strength and among the lowest unemployment rates in its history.
However, Anwar said that the ongoing conflict is beginning to affect global supply chains, particularly fuel, with oil prices rising and diesel shortages reported in several countries.
"Our biggest concern now is supply — whether essential goods, particularly fuel, will remain available — followed by price pressures," he said.
He cited disruptions overseas, including widespread petrol station closures and flight cancellations linked to fuel shortages, as indicators of potential risks.
Anwar said Malaysia has not reached such levels but cautions against complacency, urging all parties to avoid politicising the issue and instead focus on coordinated national responses.
"There are times for political differences, but this is a moment for the government, industry and the people to act as one to safeguard the economy and national interests," he said.
On domestic measures, Anwar said Putrajaya is monitoring diesel price increases and supply constraints, acknowledging their impact on key sectors despite lower overall usage compared with RON95 petrol.
He reiterated that subsidies remain in place for targeted groups, including fishermen, farmers and public transport operators.
The prime minister also said that diplomatic engagement has enabled Malaysia to secure safe passage for its oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz following direct communication with Iran's leadership.
He said one Malaysian-linked tanker has arrived in Pengerang, Johor, while another is en route, although some shipments remain affected due to ongoing hostilities.
Anwar dismissed allegations that Malaysia is exporting fuel at the expense of domestic needs, describing such claims as unfounded and harmful.
"We welcome criticism and proposals to improve policies, but misinformation and slander that undermine public confidence must stop," he said.
Anwar said the government is intensifying engagements with industry players, including small and medium enterprises, transport companies and financial institutions, to address cost pressures and ensure economic resilience amid global uncertainty.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Padini says it is unaware of wrongdoing as MACC freezes accounts
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Garment retailer Padini Holdings Bhd says it is not aware of any allegations against the company following the freezing of certain bank accounts linked to an ongoing Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) investigation involving external counterparties.
"The company wishes to emphasise that, based on information currently available, it is not aware of any allegation or wrongdoing on its part and understands that the freezing order is a procedural step taken in the course of the investigation," it said in a statement today.
Padini said certain identified bank accounts of the company and its subsidiaries were frozen on April 24 pursuant to an order issued under Section 44(1) of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities (AMLA) Act 2001.
"Based on information presently available to the company, the order was issued by the MACC in connection with an ongoing investigation involving certain external counterparties to the group," it said, adding that these parties are not employees, officers or part of the group's management.
The group said it has commenced an internal review to assess the circumstances surrounding the matter and to ensure its policies, procedures and controls remain robust and effective.
Padini also said it will continue to fully cooperate with the relevant authorities and remains committed to ensuring transparency throughout the process.
It clarified that its day-to-day operations remain fully functional and uninterrupted, with business continuing as usual.
Padini added that it has engaged external legal counsel and is taking necessary steps, including seeking appropriate relief for the unfreezing of the affected accounts.
It reiterated that it maintains a strict zero-tolerance policy against corruption and unlawful conduct and does not condone any form of bribery or improper practices.
Yesterday, Padini said in a Bursa Malaysia filing that the MACC had
frozen certain bank accounts
of Padini Holdings Bhd and its subsidiaries in connection with an ongoing investigation.
The retailer added that it continues to have access to other banking facilities to support its ongoing business operations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RoS mulls independent audit mechanism for NGOs collecting donations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Registrar of Societies (RoS) is studying proposals for guidelines and independent audits for organisations collecting public funds, following reports of alleged misappropriation involving hundreds of millions by two NGOs.
Director-general Datuk Mohd Zulfikar Ahmad said the reports have raised concerns over the transparency in the management of donations.
"RoS monitors organisations through compliance requirements, such as maintaining complete financial records, presenting financial statements at annual general meetings, and submitting documents to the department upon request.
"This monitoring covers all forms of fundraising, whether physical or through online platforms, including social media," he said in a statement.
This week, the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) uncovered two separate NGOs involved in the misappropriation of funds.
In one case, a deputy chairman of an NGO was among three people detained by the MACC for allegedly misappropriating approximately
RM230 million
, including funds linked to a zakat body.
Another case involved a freelance preacher and prominent religious influencer detained for alleged abuse of power and misappropriation of about
RM12.3 million
from an NGO for personal gain.
These two cases bring the total to at least seven NGOs involved in public fund collection, such as donations and welfare contributions, crippled by the MACC in recent months for personal use of funds.
Zulfikar said that over the past five years, up to March 31, 2026, the RoS received 314 complaints regarding alleged fund misuse by registered organisations.
During the same period, 21,013 organisations had their registrations cancelled between 2019 and 2026 for various offences, including non-compliance with legal provisions.
Zulfikar said that the RoS conducts reviews and takes action based on complaints or violations of existing laws.
"Action can include warnings, compliance notices, cancellation of registration, and referrals to other enforcement agencies, such as the police and MACC, if there are elements of criminal offences," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Only 2pct of gig workers covered: Socso warns platforms to protect workers now
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Social Security Organisation (Socso) has told platform providers to promptly align their systems and submit details of their workers to comply with the Gig Workers Act (Act 872).
In a statement today, Socso said the call comes as the current onboarding process has only covered about two per cent of the estimated 1.2 million gig workers in Malaysia, despite its digital infrastructure being fully ready.
It said the move is not merely a regulatory requirement, but a critical step to ensure consistent and continuous social protection for gig workers.
"Gig workers are constantly exposed to risks while earning a living. Any delay in system integration could affect income continuity in the event of work-related incidents," the statement read.
Socso also acknowledged concerns raised by the Malaysian E-hailing Coalition (GEM) regarding compliance with the Act, and said it is prepared to adopt stricter enforcement measures if platform providers fail to meet the requirements.
It added that platform providers must ensure social security protection for gig workers, as it is a fundamental right that supports the sustainability of the industry.
"Social security protection is not merely a legal obligation, but a responsibility to safeguard the welfare of workers who contribute to the nation's economy," Socso added.
– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Factory worker wins electric motorcycle in lucky draw
Article:
BATU KAWAN: A customer service worker won a new YADEA RS20 electric motorcycle after a lucky draw at Karnival Jom Heboh X Mega JPJ Madani 2026 in Batu Kawan.
Adriana Athirah Abdul Wahab, 23, from Nibong Tebal, did not expect her name to be announced as the grand prize winner after attending the Battle of the Band competition at Penang State Stadium last night.
She said she had originally planned to stop by the event after work to enjoy food at the Love Food Fest with her younger sibling after finishing her shift at 6pm.
"At first, we only planned to stop by briefly to eat. But for some reason, we decided to scan the QR code, enter our details and register as visitors.
"We never thought we would actually win the lucky draw for the grand prize.
"Before the concert started at around 9pm, it began to drizzle, so we decided not to go home since we had come by motorcycle. The performances were also very interesting, so we stayed and waited until the end," she said.
Adriana said she was shocked when her name was announced on stage.
"When I heard my name, I couldn't believe it. My parents don't even know yet, and they will definitely be surprised when they see the new motorcycle," she said when met late last night.
The eldest of two siblings, who works at an electrical and electronics factory in Bukit Minyak, said she had never won a lucky draw before and described the electric motorcycle as an unexpected blessing.
She added that the motorcycle would be a practical alternative, especially amid rising living costs and fluctuating fuel prices.
"It was worth stopping by Jom Heboh this time. I can't afford an electric car yet, so getting an electric motorcycle already makes me very grateful. I'm more comfortable riding a motorcycle anyway to avoid traffic jams."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Record bids for "P1P" plate as tender raises RM21.6mil
Article:
BATU KAWAN: "P1P" registration number fetched RM1.1 million in a special tender, as the "P_P" series exercise raised RM21.6 million and drew strong public participation.
Transport Minister Anthony Loke Siew Fook said the series, introduced in conjunction with the Road Transport Department's (RTD) 80th anniversary, drew participation via the JPJeBid system, reflecting public confidence in its transparent, fully digital tender process.
"The 'P1P' number fetched RM1.1 million, followed by 'P9P' at RM1,000,777, 'P8P' at RM613,000, 'P6P' at RM600,777 and 'P2P' at RM512,000.
"In total, the exercise raised RM21.68 million, comprising RM19.19 million from bids and RM2.48 million in service fees," he said at the RTD 80th anniversary celebration today.
The event was launched by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
A total of 19,370 bidders took part in the exercise, with 5,973 numbers successfully secured, while 4,026 remaining numbers will be offered to the public after the close of bidding.
Loke said no numbers were reserved or blocked, reiterating the government's commitment to openness.
Loke said the initiative was a way to boost revenue, with proceeds channelled to rakyat-centric programmes.
"My conscience is clear. We generate additional income for the government and return it to the people — whether through free helmets or helping youths obtain driving licences," he said.
The most competitive numbers in terms of participation included "P1177P" with 26 bidders, followed by "P313P" and "P22P" (24 bidders each), as well as "P411P" and "P888P" with 22 bidders each.
Loke said RTD's digitalisation efforts, including the JPJeBid platform, had improved efficiency, reduced leakages and sped up processes such as refunds, which are now credited almost immediately to unsuccessful bidders.
He said 54 per cent of RTD's RM57 million annual transactions are now conducted online via MySikap and MyJPJ, with 14.3 million registered MyJPJ users.
Integration with MyDigitalID, currently involving 6.7 million users, will soon be made mandatory as a single sign-on platform.
"Digitalisation reduces cost, enhances governance and increases revenue. It is the direction we must continue to pursue," he said.
On enforcement and integrity, Loke said RTD has implemented the ISO 37001:2016 anti-bribery management system since 2017, alongside body cameras for enforcement personnel.
He added that future plans included the adoption of automated number plate recognition (ANPR) technology integrated with highway toll systems to detect offences in real time.
Meanwhile, Loke said initiatives such as MyLesen 2026, expected to benefit 30,000 youths and B40 participants, and the distribution of 70,000 free helmets under the Madani programme, underscored the government's commitment to road safety.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Technical hurdles slow down gig platform registration with Perkeso
Article:
LETTERS: Less than one per cent of gig platforms have pre-registered with the Social Security Organisation (Perkeso), and only two platforms across the industry are fully compliant with application programming interface (API) integration requirements.
This forms the starting point for discussions on the Gig Workers Act 2025.
My Mobility Vision (MMV), a Malaysian transport and mobility policy organisation, said it convened the MMV Gig Economy Roundtable in Cyberjaya in March, bringing together operators, regulators and legal counsel to assess the implementation of Act 872.
It said discussions and subsequent integration sessions with Perkeso revealed that platforms were not unwilling to comply, but were facing incomplete systems and requirements.
During a recent session, platform representatives highlighted several issues, including the absence of a clear standard operating procedure for data sharing, whether via API, Postman or Excel.
They also raised concerns that manual deduction processes could result in double deductions for workers already contributing through full-time employment.
Several platforms reported delays in responses to API connectivity queries, which hindered integration efforts despite their willingness to comply.
Perkeso had indicated that API documentation version 2.1, required for proper integration, would be made available soon, with current requests met with instructions to wait.
MMV said these challenges reflected an ongoing system development process, with platforms required to build against evolving specifications.
The organisation also addressed remarks by gig worker advocate Masrizal Mahidin, also known as Jose Rizal, who was reported as saying that technical issues were no longer a valid excuse for non-compliance.
While acknowledging the importance of worker advocacy, MMV said such claims did not reflect the realities faced by platforms during integration.
It said the narrative that platforms were delaying compliance in bad faith overlooked key bottlenecks, including incomplete API systems, pending SOPs and ongoing regulatory development.
MMV said that gig workers are not entirely unprotected, noting that the Self-Employed Social Security Scheme, now known as Lindung Kendiri, allowed voluntary contributions at a low cost.
It said the transition under Act 872 should be viewed as a shift from optional self-contribution to mandatory platform-based deductions.
The organisation also noted that the Act was passed without a formal enforcement moratorium, with the three-to-six-month implementation window announced by the Human Resources Ministry being administrative rather than statutory.
It said effective implementation required sufficient time for system readiness, platform integration and worker awareness.
MMV reiterated that the industry supported the Gig Workers Act and recognised its role in strengthening social protection.
However, it emphasised that compliance efforts should be grounded in completed systems and clear guidelines, rather than premature enforcement.
"My Mobility Vision supports the law, but implementation must be supported by complete systems, clear SOPs and functional integration tools," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah BN unveils new leadership line-up for 2026
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Sabah Barisan Nasional has completed a major leadership restructuring for 2026.
Its chairman Datuk Seri Arthur Joseph Kurup said the new line-up blends experienced leaders with younger figures to create a more dynamic and effective party structure.
"The restructuring combines seasoned leadership with new participation, including youth representation, to ensure a more organised and responsive movement in facing current challenges," he said in a statement.
Arthur, who is also Parti Bersatu Rakyat Sabah president, remains chairman, while Sabah Umno chief Datuk Jafry Ariffin has been appointed deputy chairman.
Three vice-chairmen were named: Datuk Richard Kastum, Datuk Teah Heok Kuin and Chandrra Segar Soosaimany.
Datuk Mohd Hasnol Ayub was appointed secretary, Datuk Mohamad Alamin as treasurer, and Datuk Abdul Aziz Julkarnain as strategic communications director.
Datuk Noraini Idris was named state BN women's chief, Sufian Abd Karim will lead the Youth wing, and Ummi Nabilah Jamal the Puteri wing.
The coalition also expressed appreciation to the Sabah Unity Government machinery for its efforts during recent by-elections, crediting strong coordination and teamwork for victories in Kinabatangan (P.187) and Lamag (N.58).
Sabah BN stressed that its priority remains the full implementation of the Malaysian Agreement 1963, particularly in securing Sabah's rights within the federation.
It said it would continue taking a constructive approach to ensure all MA63 provisions are carried out comprehensively.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Anwar: Govt considers road tax cut for diesel vehicles amid cost pressure
Article:
BATU KAWAN: The government is considering reducing road tax for diesel vehicles to cushion the impact of rising fuel costs, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said.
He said the matter had been discussed with Transport Minister Anthony Loke Siew Fook, with the Transport Ministry tasked to work out the details for implementation in the near term.
"I have asked the Transport Ministry and the secretary-general to find ways so that in the near future we can reduce road tax for diesel vehicles. That can be done," he said in his speech at the Road Transport Department's 80th anniversary celebration here today.
Anwar said diesel vehicles account for about 1.8 million units nationwide — a relatively small share of the total vehicle population — but acknowledged that owners are feeling the strain from higher diesel prices.
"There are about 1.8 million diesel vehicles. Although the number is small, they are facing problems because diesel prices have gone up," he added.
Elaborating, Anwar said lowering fuel prices was not viable at present due to the heavy fiscal burden of subsidies, which costs the government about RM7 billion a month.
"That is an additional burden we have to bear. It involves public funds — taxpayers' money — and must be managed carefully," he said.
Anwar reiterated that the government would maintain its targeted subsidy approach for RON95 petrol, noting that Malaysia's fuel prices remain relatively low compared with many oil-producing countries.
"At this point, we cannot afford to reduce fuel prices further. But road tax is an area where we can provide some relief," he said.
He added that the scale of any reduction would depend on the country's fiscal position, including revenue levels and available resources.
"We have to look at the overall situation — whether we have sufficient funds and income. If there is room, we can consider further reductions," he said, adding that any proposal would be refined in consultation with the Transport Ministry.
The move is part of the government's broader effort to balance fiscal discipline with targeted assistance, particularly for groups affected by subsidy rationalisation measures.
In a press conference later, Loke said his ministry was studying options to reduce road tax for diesel vehicles, aiming to ease the burden on owners while safeguarding government revenue.
"This mandate by the Prime Minister is clear — we must help vehicle owners, particularly those affected by the current pricing structure. But any decision must take into account financial implications," he said.
Loke said that while the current fuel pricing operates on a floating mechanism with targeted subsidies, not all diesel vehicle owners qualify for assistance, which has increased cost pressures.
He said the review involved multiple considerations, including revenue trade-offs and policy alignment.
"This is not something that can be decided overnight. It involves several implications, including how much revenue the government is prepared to forgo," he added, noting that the ministry is working within a tight timeframe to finalise proposals, which will be announced in the near future.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: From assistant officer to chief commissioner: The 40-year journey of Azam Baki
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: With more than four decades of experience in anti-corruption enforcement, Tan Sri Azam Baki has built a career at the forefront of Malaysia's fight against graft.
He was appointed the sixth chief commissioner of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) on March 9, 2020, succeeding Latheefa Koya, who resigned on March 6.
Azam, 63, began his journey in public service in 1984 when he joined the Anti-Corruption Agency (now the MACC) as an assistant investigation officer.
Over the years, he rose through the ranks, serving in various capacities including investigation officer, intelligence officer and prosecuting officer.
He held several key leadership roles within the MACC, including Intelligence Division director (2013), Investigation Division director (2015), and deputy chief commissioner (Operations) (2016).
Throughout his career, he has amassed extensive experience in investigating high-profile cases reported in the media.
Among the most notable was the uncovering of the "flying licence" scandal in 2000, where he served as the investigation planner.
The operation led to the arrest of more than 100 people and the blacklisting of over 100,000 fraudulent licences.
In the aftermath of the case, the government upgraded the manual driving licence testing system to the computerised system in use today.
He is also regarded as a pioneer in the application of property forfeiture methods under key legislation, including the Anti-Corruption Act, the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission Act, and the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act.
In addition, he played a leading role in developing and planning undercover operational techniques and investigative approaches for high-profile cases.
These methods have been widely adopted by the MACC since 2013.
Azam's tenure, however, has not been without controversy.
In 2022, his ownership of nearly two million shares in a publicly listed company came to light.
However, the Anti-Corruption Advisory Board cleared him of any corruption or abuse of power.
The board said it was satisfied with his explanation that the shares had been acquired from the open market by his brother, Nasir Baki, who was using Azam's trading account at the time.
Earlier this year, he came under scrutiny again following Bloomberg's reports regarding his shareholdings in a financial services firm.
Azam was listed as holding 17.7 million shares in Velocity Capital Partner Bhd, citing a corporate filing with the Companies Commission of Malaysia (SSM) on Feb 3 last year.
This raised questions about compliance with civil service regulations.
Under the Public Service Human Resource Management Circular (MyPPSM) Ceraian UP.7.2.6 on the ownership and declaration of assets by public officers, a senior civil servant's permissible equity interest is assessed using a dual-limb test: it must not exceed either five per cent of a company's paid-up capital or RM100,000 in current value, whichever is lower.
Azam later clarified that his shareholding had been conducted transparently and in accordance with regulations, adding that the shares were purchased and disposed of within the same year.
Azam's contract is set to end on May 12.
Earlier today, the government announced that
Datuk Seri Abdul Halim Aman
, a former High Court judge with nearly two decades of judicial experience and a long career in the legal service, had been appointed the new MACC chief commissioner.
His appointment takes effect on May 13.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: New MACC chief appointment a test for the system, says watchdog
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Public confidence in the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) will hinge on its new leadership's handling of high-profile cases, says an anti-graft watchdog.
Malaysia Corruption Watch (MCW) president Jais Abdul Karim said the appointment of Datuk Seri Abdul Halim Aman is a litmus test for the nation's institutional framework rather than a mere change in leadership.
"Public confidence will only be restored if this new leadership can act decisively on high-profile cases without fear or favour," he said in a statement.
"We view this appointment as a significant step in strengthening the anti-corruption institution, particularly in the current climate, which demands integrity, transparency, and institutional courage."
Earlier today, Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar announced Halim's appointment.
The former High Court judge's tenure is effective May 13. He replaces Tan Sri Azam Baki, who has led the agency since 2020.
Jais said that aside from handling high-profile cases, the new MACC leadership must also ensure enhanced transparency in investigation processes, strengthen internal integrity, and advocate for structural reforms, including the separation of investigative and prosecutorial powers.
He warned that the commission continues to face significant hurdles, including political pressure and public perceptions of selective prosecution.
Jais added that a shift in the organisation's internal culture is necessary to ensure long-term effectiveness.
"If the principles of integrity are upheld, public confidence will return. If not, this will be viewed simply as a change of face without real reform," he said.
Azam has faced numerous calls to step down over share ownership and allegations of links to a purported "corporate mafia". He has denied any wrongdoing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran's foreign minister arrives in Pakistan, raising hopes for US peace talks
Article:
ISLAMABAD: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi arrived in the Pakistani capital Islamabad on Friday to discuss proposals for restarting peace talks with the United States, amid conflicting signals on whether he would meet with US negotiators there.
Pakistani sources said Araqchi was not slated to meet US negotiators in Islamabad, while White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said special envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner would leave for Pakistan on Saturday morning for talks with the Iranian official.
Pakistan's foreign minister confirmed the arrival of the Iranian delegation, saying Araqchi would meet with senior Pakistani officials to discuss the peace efforts.
Qatar's Emir Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani discussed developments in the Washington-Tehran ceasefire agreement in a phone call with US President Donald Trump, Qatar's state news agency reported late on Friday.
The Qatari leader said his country would continue coordinating with partners to support mediation efforts led by Pakistan.
Islamabad was the planned venue for US-Iran talks that failed to materialise earlier this week.
Leavitt struck an upbeat tone, saying the US had seen some progress from the Iranian side in recent days and hoped more would come this weekend.
She added that US Vice President JD Vance, who earlier this month led a first round of unsuccessful talks with Iran to end their war, is ready to travel to Pakistan to join the negotiations if they prove successful.
Araqchi wrote on X that he was visiting Pakistan, Oman and Russia to coordinate with partners on bilateral matters and consult on regional developments, adding that Iran's neighbours remained Teheran's priority.
The tour will include consultations on the latest efforts to end the war, Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesperson later told state media.
Two Pakistani government sources aware of the discussions said Araqchi's visit would be brief and focus on Iran's proposals for talks with the US, which mediator Pakistan would then convey to Washington.
US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth told a briefing earlier on Friday that Iran had a chance to make a "good deal" with the United States.
"Iran knows that they still have an open window to choose wisely ... at the negotiating table. All they have to do is abandon a nuclear weapon in meaningful and verifiable ways," he said.
Reports on Araqchi's trip in Iranian state media and the Pakistani sources made no mention of Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, the speaker of Iran's parliament, who was the head of its delegation at the talks earlier this month.
The Iranian parliament's media office denied a report that Qalibaf had resigned as head of Iran's negotiating team, and added that there was no new round of talks scheduled yet.
Pakistani sources said earlier that a US logistics and security team already was in place in Islamabad for potential talks.
The last round of peace talks had been expected to resume on Tuesday but never took place, with Iran saying it was not yet ready to commit to attending and a US delegation led by Vance never leaving Washington.
Trump unilaterally extended a two-week ceasefire on Tuesday at the 11th hour to allow more time to reconvene the negotiators.
Oil prices remained volatile on Friday, as traders weighed potential disruption from the worst oil shock in history amid the prospect for further talks.
Brent crude futures were largely unchanged at US$105.11 a barrel while US West Texas Intermediate futures were down 1.7 per cent to US$94.23.
Hizbollah dismisses Lebanon ceasefire extension
On Thursday, Israel and Lebanon extended a separate ceasefire for three weeks at a meeting at the White House brokered by Trump.
The war in Lebanon, which Israel invaded last month to root out Iran's Hezbollah allies after the militant group fired across the border, has run in parallel with the wider Iran war, and Teheran says a ceasefire there is a precondition for talks.
There was little sign of an end to the fighting in southern Lebanon, however, as Lebanese authorities reported two people were killed by an Israeli strike and Hizbollah downed an Israeli drone.
While the ceasefire that came into force on April 16 has led to a significant reduction in hostilities, Israel and Hizbollah have continued to trade blows in southern Lebanon, where Israel has kept soldiers in a self-declared "buffer zone."
Responding to the extension, Hezbollah lawmaker Ali Fayyad said, "It is essential to point out that the ceasefire is meaningless in light of Israel's insistence on hostile acts, including assassinations, shelling, and gunfire" and its demolition of villages and towns in the south.
Israel's military said it had killed six armed Hizbollah members in southern Lebanon on Friday.
Strait of Hormuz blockade
Trump said on Thursday he was in no rush to reach an agreement with Iran and wanted it to be "everlasting," while asserting the US had an upper hand in the standoff in the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most important energy shipping route.
The US has yet to find a way to open the strait, where Iran has blocked nearly all ships apart from its own since the start of the war eight weeks ago. Iran showed off its control this week by seizing two huge cargo vessels there.
Trump imposed a separate blockade of Iranian shipping last week. Iran says it will not reopen the strait until Trump lifts his blockade.
Only five ships crossed the strait in the last 24 hours, shipping data showed on Friday, compared to around 130 a day before the war.
Those included one Iranian oil products tanker, but none of the vast crude-carrying supertankers that normally feed global energy markets.
Container shipping company Hapag-Lloyd also said one of its ships had crossed the strait, without giving details. — REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman arrested for allegedly sparking bushfire through open burning
Article:
BACHOK: Police have arrested a woman suspected of causing a bushfire at Jalan Pantai Perkasa here yesterday.
District police chief Superintendent Mohamad Ismail Jamaluddin said police were alerted to the incident at 4.30pm.
"According to a witness, the complainant alerted him to a fire in the bushes. Together with other villagers, they rushed to the scene.
"The flames had intensified by the time they arrived and were spreading towards the area near the complainant's house," he said.
He added that the villagers managed to extinguish the fire before it could cause further damage, including to an abandoned house nearby.
Ismail said the affected area covered open land between the abandoned house and the front of the complainant's residence.
"The fire damaged coconut trees, a car, construction timber, and an iron fence.
"No closed-circuit television (CCTV) cameras were found at the location," he said.
He added that police found signs of rubbish burning on land belonging to the suspect.
"Based on witness statements, the suspect was seen carrying out open burning around 11.30am on the day of the incident," he said.
The woman has been detained to assist in investigations under Section 285 of the Penal Code for negligent conduct with fire or combustible matter endangering property.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: King consents to Abdul Halim's appointment as new MACC chief
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, has consented to the appointment of former High Court judge Datuk Seri Abdul Halim Aman as the new chief commissioner of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC), effective May 13.
Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar said the appointment is in line with Subsection 5(1) of the MACC Act 2009 (Act 694).
"The government is confident that, with his extensive experience and high integrity, he will be able to strengthen governance, enhance public confidence and intensify anti-corruption efforts in the interest of the nation," he said in a statement today.
Abdul Halim's appointment will replace Tan Sri Azam Baki, whose tenure is scheduled to end on May 12.
Yesterday, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim submitted a list of candidates for the MACC chief commissioner post to His Majesty.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Poodle abuse: Animal welfare group calls for help to catch suspect
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Stray Animal Association of Malaysia (SAFM) has condemned the viral abuse of a poodle in Seri Kembangan, calling on members of the public with information on the person responsible to assist investigators immediately.
The association said it viewed the case, in which a poodle was allegedly hurled against a wall, sustaining injuries, with the utmost seriousness.
"Animals also feel pain, fear, and trauma," SAFM said in a post on Facebook yesterday.
The association urged anyone with information to lodge a report with the Selangor Veterinary Services Department, whose investigating officer can be reached at 013-432 1512.
Members of the public can also channel information directly to SAFM at 013-706 6770.
SAFM called on the public to help identify the suspect and channel information to the authorities to put a stop to violence against animals.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elderly man flung off bike, killed after two motorcycles collide
Article:
SHAH ALAM: An elderly man was killed and two others injured when two motorcycles collided at Sungai Kamin, Hulu Selangor, last night.
Hulu Selangor police chief Superintendent Ibrahim Husin said the crash, which occurred shortly before midnight, involved a Honda EX-5 and a Yamaha Ego.
"Initial investigations indicate the 74-year-old victim, who was riding the Honda, was heading towards an eatery.
"As he was turning right, a Yamaha Ego carrying a 21-year-old man and a 14-year-old girl from the opposite direction crashed into his motorcycle," Ibrahim said when contacted today.
The impact flung the elderly man and his motorcycle onto the road shoulder. The second motorcycle crashed into a nearby lamp post.
The victim suffered severe head injuries and died at the scene. The other motorcyclist and his pillion rider sustained injuries to their legs and hands.
Those with information are urged to contact traffic investigating officer Inspector Mohammadi Mt Abu @ Ramli at 03-60641222.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC: Lapses in oversight enable NGO fund misuse
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Investigations by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) have found that some non-governmental organisations (NGOs) collecting public donations have deviated from their original objectives, including using funds to amass extraordinary wealth and purchase luxury items.
Many were found to have misused funds for personal gain, involving not only top management but also members and certain individuals within the organisations.
These groups typically exploit loopholes in the NGO oversight system to generate easy wealth, given the large sums collected from donors.
In some cases, their methods go beyond creating deceptive donation systems, with such activities becoming a primary source of income.
As a result, chairmen, directors and their associates are seen to possess unusual levels of wealth and extensive luxury assets, shocking the public when such cases are exposed by MACC.
MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said such practices were commonly detected among NGOs under investigation, where weaknesses such as the lack of effective monitoring by relevant authorities, including the Registrar of Societies (RoS), were manipulated.
He said these gaps in oversight have enabled abuse to persist, underscoring the need for stricter governance and enforcement.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Billionaire Elon Musk enters courtroom showdown with OpenAI
Article:
SAN FRANCISCO: Jury selection is to begin Monday in a high-profile legal battle between billionaire Elon Musk and artificial intelligence startup OpenAI, which he accuses of betraying its non-profit mission.
The clash in a courtroom across the bay from San Francisco pits the world's richest man against a startup that Musk once backed and now competes against in the booming AI sector.
OpenAI's ChatGPT is a formidable rival to the Grok chatbot made by Musk's xAI lab.
While the lawsuit filed by Musk is part of a feud between him and OpenAI chief executive Sam Altman, it spotlights a debate on whether AI should ultimately benefit the privileged few or society as a whole.
Court filings lay out how Altman tried to convince Musk to back OpenAI in 2015, acting as a co-founder for a non-profit lab whose technology "would belong to the world."
Musk pumped some US$38 million into the lab before he left.
OpenAI is now valued at US$852 billion, with Microsoft among its backers, and is preparing to go public on the stock market.
The judge presiding over the trial is aiming for a jury to decide by late May whether OpenAI broke a promise to Musk in its drive to be a leader in AI or just smartly rode the technology to glory.
Musk duped?
Musk argues in his lawsuit that he was deceived about OpenAI's mission being altruistic.
The tycoon cites an email from Altman in 2017 claiming that he remained "enthusiastic about the non-profit structure" of their AI venture after Musk threatened to cut off funding for the lab.
Just a few months later, however, OpenAI established a commercial subsidiary in the face of needing to invest hundreds of billions of dollars in data centres to power its technology.
Over the course of the following two years, Microsoft pumped billions of dollars into OpenAI and the tech stalwart's stake in the startup is now valued about US$135 billion.
Microsoft chief executive Satya Nadella is among those slated to testify at the trial.
Aimed at Altman
Along with calling for OpenAI to be forced to revert to a pure nonprofit, Musk's suit urges the ousting of Altman and OpenAI co-founder and president Greg Brockman.
Musk is also seeking as much as US$134 billion in damages and to have the court make OpenAI sever ties with Microsoft.
During pre-trial hearings, US Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers mused that Musk's team seemed to be "pulling numbers out of the air" when it came to calculating damages.
If the jury sides with Musk, it will be left to Rogers to determine any remedies or payment.
In what OpenAI has dismissed as a public relations stunt, Musk has vowed that any damages awarded in the suit will go to the startup's non-profit foundation.
Quest for control?
OpenAI internal communications brought to light by the lawsuit reveal tensions that culminated in the temporary ouster of Altman as AI chief executive in late 2023.
Musk's legal team highlighted a 2017 entry in Brockman's personal journal reasoning that it would be lying if Altman publicly asserted OpenAI would stay a non-profit but became a corporation a short time later.
OpenAI now has a hybrid governance structure giving its non-profit foundation control over a for-profit arm.
In court filings, OpenAI countered that its break-up with Musk was due to his quest for absolute control rather than its non-profit status.
"This case has always been about Elon generating more power and more money for what he wants," OpenAI said in a post on X, a platform Musk owns.
"His lawsuit remains nothing more than a harassment campaign that's driven by ego, jealousy and a desire to slow down a competitor."
The startup noted that days after Musk entered the AI race in 2023 he called for a six-month moratorium on development of advanced AI.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: What we know about new MACC chief Abdul Halim Aman
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Datuk Seri Abdul Halim Aman, a former High Court judge with nearly two decades of judicial experience and a long career in the legal service, has been appointed the new Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner.
His appointment, announced on Friday, takes effect on May 13 following the end of Tan Sri Azam Baki's contract.
Born on March 10, 1957, in Kampung Alai, Malacca, Halim obtained his LLB (Hons) from the Universiti Malaya in 1982 and later completed his LLM at the University of Hull, United Kingdom in 1996.
According to the Malaysia Judiciary Yearbook 2023, he began his career in the Judicial and Legal Service in 1982, serving in various capacities over 22 years, including Federal Counsel at the Attorney General's Chambers, magistrate posts in Kluang and Muar, senior assistant registrar at the Malacca High Court, and Sessions Court judge in multiple locations including Tawau, Sandakan, Kuala Lumpur, George Town and Ampang.
He also served as Senior Federal Counsel with the Inland Revenue Board and the Attorney General's Chambers, as well as Pahang State Legal Adviser, deputy public prosecutor, and Head of the Research Division at the Office of the Chief Justice.
In 2005, he was appointed Judicial Commissioner at the Temerloh High Court before being transferred to Kuantan in 2006.
He was elevated to High Court Judge in 2007.
He later served at the Penang High Court (2009) and Johor Baru High Court (2010) before his long tenure at the Shah Alam High Court from 2013 until his mandatory retirement in March 2023.
During his time in Shah Alam, he presided over several high-profile criminal cases that drew public attention.
Among them was the 2015 murder trial involving a TV9 administrator, where he acquitted the accused after ruling that the prosecution's investigation and forensic evidence were inadequate, stressing the importance of proper investigative procedures.
In 2019, he sentenced three people to death in the Klang "body-less" murder case, describing the crime as "vicious, brutal and inhumane."
That same year, he rejected an appeal by preacher Wan Ji Wan Hussin in a sedition case involving comments on the Sultan of Selangor, and enhanced the sentence to one year in prison, citing loyalty to the Rukun Negara principle of allegiance to the Ruler.
In 2021, he sentenced a babysitter's husband to death for the murder of an 11-month-old infant, describing the act as "heartless and inhumane."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japanese fugitive murder suspect nabbed after seven years hiding in Malaysia [WATCH[
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A Japanese fugitive wanted for murder in his home country has been deported after evading authorities for seven years, in a case that underscores Malaysia's growing role in global crime enforcement cooperation.
Police confirmed the suspect, who had been on the Interpol Red Notice list, was tracked down and detained in Malaysia before being handed over to Japanese authorities.
Bukit Aman Criminal Investigation Department director Datuk M. Kumar said the arrest was the result of close coordination between Malaysia and Japan, led by the police's Interpol division.
"The operation reflects our continued commitment to combating cross-border crime and strengthening international security cooperation," he said in a statement.
He said investigations revealed the suspect had been living under a false identity, posing as a Myanmar national to evade detection.
"He was initially arrested in Kedah for separate offences, including drug-related charges and immigration violations, before authorities uncovered his true identity.
"Forensic and scientific analysis, including fingerprint comparison, facial recognition and DNA profiling, confirmed a match with records provided by Japanese authorities," he said.
Kumar said the suspect had previously been charged at the Alor Setar court in August last year and served prison sentences for multiple offences under Malaysian law before his deportation.
He stressed that the successful identification and extradition send a clear message that Malaysia will not serve as a safe haven for international fugitives.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Azam: NGOs cannot take 30pct of public donations as commission
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has clarified that non-governmental organisations (NGOs) are not permitted to take 30 per cent of public donations as income or commission, describing such belief as incorrect.
MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the misconception stems from confusion over governance guidelines issued by the Registrar of Societies (ROS), which state that administrative costs should generally not exceed 30 per cent of an organisation's annual expenditure.
He said the figure refers strictly to operational or overhead expenses such as salaries, office rental, utilities, programme management and other administrative costs — not a share of public donations for personal gain.
"This 30 per cent figure does not mean that NGOs or their founders can take 30 per cent of public donations as commission or profit.
"For example, if an NGO's monthly administrative costs — including salaries, rent and utilities — amount to RM30,000, only RM9,000 should be allocated for administrative purposes under the guideline," he told Harian Metro.
Azam said that even if an NGO receives millions in public donations, the guideline is applied to the organisation's overall annual expenditure structure, not the total amount of funds raised.
"The condition is 30 per cent of annual expenditure, not 30 per cent of total donations.
"The figure serves as a guideline to ensure administrative spending does not exceed programme-related expenditure."
He said the belief that NGOs are entitled to take a fixed percentage of public donations is misleading.
Azam also warned that a lack of transparency in administrative spending, proper financial reporting or auditing could expose organisations to risks such as fund misappropriation, conflicts of interest or criminal breach of trust.
"Therefore, the key principles in NGO fund management are accountability, transparent financial reporting and continuous monitoring by the relevant authorities.
"Firm action must be taken if there are suspicions, especially clear breaches of regulations," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Humanitarian scam: NGOs allegedly profited from images of Palestinian war victims
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Exploiting the suffering of war victims, some non-governmental organisations (NGOs) are believed to be using harrowing images of destruction to solicit public donations—only for the funds to be misappropriated for personal gain.
Emotional narratives highlighting the plight of Palestinians and victims of other conflicts are often amplified through graphic videos and images to stir sympathy.
Moved by these appeals, the public donates generously, believing their contributions will reach those in desperate need.
However, concerns have emerged that in certain cases, these funds are instead diverted, with little transparency or accountability from the organisations involved.
Some NGOs are also believed to have submitted claims for humanitarian programmes—such as food distribution efforts—that were never carried out.
These fabricated initiatives serve as a modus operandi to siphon off public donations.
Revealing these patterns, Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said investigations into such cases involve detailed scrutiny of financial flows, accounting records, transaction documents, and management decisions within the organisations.
He said probes typically focus on identifying elements of offences such as abuse of power, false claims, and money laundering.
"In several cases reported previously, investigations found specific modus operandi used by irresponsible parties to misuse NGO funds.
"Among the methods identified were transferring public donation funds into personal accounts or company accounts linked to NGO management, submitting claims for programmes that were never implemented, and using donated funds to purchase personal assets such as property, vehicles, or other investments," he told Harian Metro.
In one case uncovered by the commission, public donations amounting to approximately RM26 million were allegedly
channelled into personal accounts
and used to acquire assets including houses, land, and even cryptocurrency.
Azam added that in certain instances, NGO funds were redirected to companies with vested interests connected to individuals within the organisation's leadership.
"This can create conflicts of interest and may involve offences under laws related to corruption and criminal breach of trust."
He said there are also cases involving false claims of welfare programmes that were never carried out despite funds being allocated.
"The MACC will conduct thorough investigations, including examining financial transactions, accounting documents, and obtaining witness statements to determine whether any elements of misappropriation or misuse of funds have occurred."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US says two killed in boat strike as toll climbs over 180
Article:
WASHINGTON, (United States): The US military said it killed two people in a strike on an alleged drug-trafficking vessel Friday, as the death toll of Washington's campaign against "narco-terrorists" in Latin America climbed to at least 182.
The military "conducted a lethal kinetic strike on a vessel operated by Designated Terrorist Organisations," the US Southern Command, which is responsible for Washington's forces in the region, said in an X post.
"Intelligence confirmed the vessel was transiting along known narco-trafficking routes in the Eastern Pacific and was engaged in narco-trafficking operations," it added, echoing language it has used to describe dozens of deadly operations since the campaign began last September.
US military officials have claimed at least seven such strikes in April, bringing the total toll of people killed in these operations at least 182, according to an AFP tally.
The Trump administration has provided no definitive evidence that the vessels it targets are involved in drug trafficking, prompting debate about the legality of the operations.
International legal experts and rights groups say the strikes likely amount to extrajudicial killings as they have apparently targeted civilians who do not pose an immediate threat to the United States. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: What we know about new MACC chief Abdul Halim Aman
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 13 — After the announcement of Datuk Seri Abdul Halim Aman as the new Chief Commissioner of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC), Malaysians will likely be curious about Abdul Halim’s background.
His appointment, made under subsection 5(1) of the MACC Act (Act 694), received the consent of His Majesty the Yang di-Pertuan Agong, Sultan Ibrahim.
Abdul Halim brings to his new role decades of judicial experience.
A former High Court judge, Abdul Halim retired from the bench in 2023 after nearly two decades of service.
According to Bernama, Abdul Halim holds a Bachelor of Laws (Honours) degree from the University of Malaya and a Master of Laws from the University of Hull in the United Kingdom.
His judicial career began in 2005 when he was appointed a Judicial Commissioner. He was elevated to High Court Judge in 2007, subsequently serving in the Kuantan, Johor Bahru, and Shah Alam High Courts before his mandatory retirement in 2023.
Yang di-Pertuan Agong Sultan Ibrahim telah memperkenan pelantikan bekas Hakim Mahkamah Tinggi Datuk Seri Abdul Halim Aman sebagai Ketua Pesuruhjaya Suruhanjaya Pencegahan Rasuah Malaysia (SPRM) yang baharu, berkuat kuasa 13 Mei.
